project.name: ruvector (slice 1 — CLI install path)
project.description: Slice 1 of /home/drdave/repos/RuVector. Scope: what `cargo install ruvector-cli` delivers — top-level project files, the ruvector-cli binary crate, the ruvector-core library it depends on, the npm wrapper root, and the install.sh installer. The full RuVector repo (135 crates) is intentionally out of scope here; subsequent slices will cover mcp/cognitum-gate/ruvllm/rvAgent/etc.

nodes.name: .dockerignore
nodes.summary: Docker build context exclusion list preventing target/, node_modules/, and other large or sensitive directories from being shipped into images.

nodes.name: .env.example
nodes.summary: Template environment file documenting required secrets and runtime variables (API keys for crates.io, npm, Anthropic, OpenAI, HuggingFace and Claude-Flow tuning knobs) to be copied to .env.

nodes.name: .gcloudignore
nodes.summary: Google Cloud deployment ignore manifest used when pushing source via gcloud, mirroring .gitignore plus build artifacts.

nodes.name: .gitmodules
nodes.summary: Git submodule registry declaring external repositories pulled into the RuVector tree.

nodes.name: intelligence.json
nodes.summary: Persisted self-learning state for the RuVector hooks engine — captures patterns, memories, trajectories, agents, edges, and stats accumulated across sessions.
nodes.languageNotes: Single 5.2K-line JSON document acting as the brain snapshot — heavy nested arrays keyed by top-level sections (patterns, memories, trajectories, agents, edges, stats).

nodes.name: CHANGELOG.md
nodes.summary: Versioned release log spanning 0.1.0 through the hailo-backend release, documenting added features, performance improvements, fixes, and breaking changes per release.

nodes.name: CLAUDE.md
nodes.summary: Claude Code configuration and behavioral playbook for working in RuVector — covers swarm orchestration rules, 3-tier model routing, CLI commands, memory operations, and the pi.ruv.io brain integration.

nodes.name: Cargo.toml
nodes.summary: Root Cargo workspace manifest declaring all RuVector member crates, shared workspace dependencies, release/bench/dev/test profiles, and crates-io patch overrides.
nodes.languageNotes: Workspace-level Cargo.toml with extensive [workspace.dependencies] section centralising version pins for every member crate; release profile is hand-tuned for size + LTO.

nodes.name: README.md
nodes.summary: Canonical 5.5K-line project overview describing RuVector as a self-learning vector + graph + LLM operating system — covers GNN mechanics, quick start, ecosystem packages, performance benchmarks, deployment, and tutorials.

nodes.name: install.sh
nodes.summary: One-line installer bootstrapping Rust (rustup) if missing, then running `cargo install ruvector-cli` and `npm install -g ruvector` plus printing the catalog of available library crates and uninstall instructions.
nodes.languageNotes: Pure bash with platform detection (Linux/macOS/Windows-WSL on x86_64/arm64), pretty-printed step output, and graceful degradation when cargo or npm are absent.

nodes.name: detect_platform
nodes.summary: Detects host OS (Linux/macOS/Windows) and architecture (x86_64/aarch64), exiting with an error for unsupported targets.

nodes.name: check_dependencies
nodes.summary: Verifies presence of required tools (curl, git, cargo, node, npm) and warns the user about anything missing before attempting installs.

nodes.name: install_rust
nodes.summary: Installs the Rust toolchain via rustup.rs if cargo is not already present, sourcing the resulting environment for the rest of the script.

nodes.name: install_cli
nodes.summary: Runs `cargo install ruvector-cli` to publish the binary into ~/.cargo/bin, streaming progress and falling back to an already-installed check on failure.

nodes.name: install_npm
nodes.summary: Optionally installs the global `ruvector` npm package when Node.js is available, surfacing a sudo hint if the install fails due to permissions.

nodes.name: show_crates
nodes.summary: Prints the catalog of RuVector library crates available on crates.io as informational guidance after the CLI is installed.

nodes.name: show_npm
nodes.summary: Prints the npm package catalog (ruvector-cnn, rvf, pi-brain, etc.) so users know which JS/TS packages they can install separately.

nodes.name: show_uninstall
nodes.summary: Prints the uninstall commands for the CLI and npm packages so users have a clean rollback path.

nodes.name: main
nodes.summary: Top-level installer orchestrator — parses CLI flags, prints the banner, runs platform detection, optionally installs Rust, runs the cargo + npm installs, and prints success/uninstall guidance.

nodes.name: .prettierrc.json

nodes.name: npm/README.md

nodes.name: VERIFICATION_COMPLETE.md

tour.title: Project Intent & Manifesto
tour.description: Start with the canonical project pitch. README.md is the 5.5K-line overview that frames RuVector as a self-learning vector + graph + LLM operating system (GNN that learns from queries, SONA self-optimizer, DiskANN, ColBERT, Matryoshka, hybrid sparse+dense, Cypher, local LLMs). CLAUDE.md is the in-repo playbook for agents working in the tree, and CHANGELOG.md captures what has actually shipped through the 0.1.x and hailo-backend releases — together they tell you what the workstation install is supposed to deliver and at what maturity.

tour.title: The Installer's Contract
tour.description: `install.sh` is the user-facing front door this slice is about: a one-line bootstrap that installs Rust via rustup if missing, then runs `cargo install ruvector-cli` (producing the Rust binaries) plus `npm install -g ruvector` (the JS wrapper). The Cargo workspace manifest declares which member crates exist and the shared dependency versions, so reading the two together tells you exactly which artifacts land on disk and from where.
tour.languageLesson: Cargo workspaces let multiple crates share one Cargo.lock and one target/ directory. `cargo install <crate>` reads the crate's [[bin]] entries from its own Cargo.toml and compiles each as a standalone binary into ~/.cargo/bin — that's how a single workspace publishes both `ruvector` and `ruvector-mcp-server`.

tour.title: What `cargo install ruvector-cli` Actually Builds
tour.description: The ruvector-cli crate manifest is the precise specification of what gets compiled and installed. It declares two binaries — `ruvector` and `ruvector-mcp-server` — pulls in ~60 dependencies (clap, tokio, sqlx, axum, indicatif), and gates optional features. After this step you can predict exactly which executables show up in `~/.cargo/bin` and what optional capabilities the build can toggle.
tour.languageLesson: Cargo `[[bin]]` tables are how one crate publishes multiple binaries; each entry maps a name to a `path` source file. Feature flags in `[features]` let downstream installers opt into heavier code paths (e.g. postgres, hailo) without bloating the default build.

tour.title: Binary Entry Point & Command Dispatch
tour.description: `main.rs` is the literal entry point of the `ruvector` binary you just installed: it defines the top-level clap Cli/Commands enum and dispatches every subcommand to a handler in the `cli/` module. `config.rs` is the very first thing those handlers consult — a hierarchical Config / DatabaseConfig / CliConfig / McpConfig loaded from TOML and overridden by env vars. Together they answer 'when I type `ruvector …`, what runs and how does it find its settings?'
tour.languageLesson: Clap's derive API turns a Rust enum into a full argument parser at compile time: each variant becomes a subcommand and its fields become named flags. The dispatcher is a single `match` on the enum — adding a verb means adding a variant and one match arm, nothing more.

tour.title: The CLI Command Surface — What You'll Actually Type
tour.description: These modules implement the verbs a user runs after install. `commands.rs` owns the core vector-DB ops (create, insert, search, info, benchmark, export, import) including JSON/CSV/NPY ingestion. `graph.rs` adds the Cypher subcommands and an embedded HTTP/gRPC/GraphQL server. `format.rs` and `progress.rs` are the shared output layer (colored tables, JSON/CSV dumps, indicatif progress bars). Read this step to know which commands actually exist on the binary that just landed in your PATH.

tour.title: Self-Learning Hooks & PostgreSQL State
tour.description: RuVector's claim to fame is that it learns from how you use it. The hooks subsystem is where that happens: `hooks.rs` is a Q-learning engine over patterns, vector-embedded memories, trajectories, error sequences, and file-sequence prediction; `hooks_postgres.rs` is the pgvector-backed persistence layer; and `hooks_schema.sql` is the actual eight-table schema (`ruvector_hooks_patterns`, `_memories`, `_trajectories`, `_errors`, `_file_sequences`, `_swarm_agents`, `_swarm_edges`, `_stats`) that the install writes when you opt into the Postgres backend.
tour.languageLesson: pgvector extends Postgres with a `vector` column type and ANN operators (`<=>` cosine, `<#>` inner product, `<->` L2). It lets the hooks engine store learned memories as embeddings directly in SQL and recall them with the same WHERE-clause language used for regular columns — no separate vector store required.

tour.title: The Embedded MCP Server
tour.description: The install ships a second binary, `ruvector-mcp-server`, that exposes RuVector as a Model Context Protocol endpoint so Claude and other LLM clients can call it as a tool. `mcp_server.rs` is the binary entry point. The `mcp/` module is the implementation: `protocol.rs` defines JSON-RPC and MCP wire types; `handlers.rs` dispatches initialize / tools/list / tools/call and the underlying create_db / insert / search tool handlers; `transport.rs` provides both stdio and an axum-based SSE/HTTP server; `gnn_cache.rs` is an LRU cache of GNN forward passes that keeps tool calls fast.
tour.languageLesson: MCP rides on JSON-RPC 2.0: every request is a `{jsonrpc, id, method, params}` envelope, every response carries either a `result` or an `error`. Servers advertise capabilities in `initialize`, then expose tools via `tools/list` and execute them via `tools/call` — the same handler can be reached over stdio (for spawned subprocesses) or over SSE/HTTP (for networked clients).

tour.title: The npm Wrapper Path (`npx ruvector`)
tour.description: Beyond `cargo install`, the installer also publishes an npm package so Node users can run `npx ruvector` or `npm install -g ruvector` instead. The npm root workspace manifest declares the wrapper packages and lifecycle scripts; the user-facing npm/README documents the JS API (VectorDB class, RAG/cypher bindings) and how the package ships the WASM and the native CLI; PUBLISHING_STATUS tracks readiness of `ruvector`, `@ruvector/core`, and `@ruvector/wasm` across the three-phase rollout.

tour.title: The Library Underneath: `ruvector-core` Public API
tour.description: Both the CLI and the npm wrapper sit on top of `ruvector-core`. `lib.rs` is the crate root and the highest-fan-out node in the graph — it declares every top-level module and re-exports the public API. `types.rs` defines the vocabulary (DistanceMetric, VectorEntry, SearchQuery, SearchResult, DbOptions, HnswConfig, QuantizationConfig). `vector_db.rs` is the high-level façade — a VectorDB type that composes storage + index + quantization into one CRUD-and-search object. This is the API surface the CLI command handlers in Step 5 actually call into.
tour.languageLesson: Rust's `lib.rs` plays the role of both the module tree root and the public-API gate: anything not `pub` from `lib.rs` is invisible to other crates. The pattern of re-exporting (`pub use module::Thing`) keeps the implementation modular while presenting consumers with a flat, stable surface.

tour.title: Vector-DB Primitives — How Search Actually Runs
tour.description: These are the building blocks the VectorDB façade composes. `distance.rs` is the scalar reference for Euclidean / cosine / dot; `simd_intrinsics.rs` is the same math vectorised across AVX2, AVX2-FMA, AVX-512 and NEON. `index.rs` declares the VectorIndex trait, and `hnsw.rs` is the production implementation — a Hierarchical Navigable Small World graph giving logarithmic-time ANN search with persistence. `storage.rs` is the crash-safe append-only on-disk format. `quantization.rs` provides Scalar/Int8, Product, Int4, and Binary quantized vectors with SIMD-accelerated distances. `embeddings.rs` is the EmbeddingProvider abstraction (local deterministic hash embedder + HTTP API embedder for OpenAI/Cohere).
tour.languageLesson: Rust SIMD is usually written behind `#[cfg(target_feature = …)]` guards plus runtime CPU detection: you compile every kernel (scalar, AVX2, AVX-512, NEON) into the binary and dispatch at runtime via `is_x86_feature_detected!`. That's how a single installed binary can light up AVX-512 on a Sapphire Rapids box and NEON on an M-series Mac without recompilation.

tour.title: The Advanced Features That Make This Not a Toy
tour.description: The differentiators advertised in Step 1 live here. `advanced_features.rs` is the module root. `diskann.rs` is a DiskANN/Vamana graph index for billion-scale on-disk ANN. `hybrid_search.rs` fuses BM25 keyword scores with vector similarity using configurable score normalization. `graph_rag.rs` is the GraphRAG pipeline (entity/relation extraction, community detection, local/global/hybrid retrieval). `matryoshka.rs` is multi-resolution embedding search that funnels from short to full-dim. After install, every one of these is reachable through the CLI verbs in Step 5 and the MCP tool calls in Step 7.

tour.title: Verification Surface — Did the Install Actually Work?
tour.description: Closing the loop on the slice's stated purpose: how do you check the install? `cli_tests.rs` is the assert_cmd-driven smoke suite that exercises `--version`, `--help`, create, info, insert-from-json, search, and benchmark — exactly the commands you'd run by hand. `mcp_tests.rs` validates the JSON-RPC wire format of the embedded MCP server from Step 7. `integration_tests.rs` is the end-to-end VectorDB workflow (insert/search round-trips, 10k-vector batches, persistence reload). `hnsw_search.rs` is the Criterion benchmark you can run to confirm the SIMD kernels from Step 10 are firing on your CPU. Together they constitute the verification surface for `cargo install ruvector-cli`.
